title: Ipomoea corralinensis
taxonomy: Ipomoea corralinensis is a species of morning glory, classified within the family Convolvulaceae. This family of flowering plants consists of over 1,800 species, with Convolvulaceae being one of the largest families in the angiosperm clade. The genus Ipomoea, to which Ipomoea corralinensis belongs, comprises approximately 700 species of annual and perennial plants. Ipomoea corralinensis is native to tropical regions and is characterized by its climbing habit and showy flowers. The exact classification of Ipomoea corralinensis may vary depending on the source and taxonomic system used.
uses: Ipomoea corralinensis is a plant with potential medicinal uses, particularly in traditional medicine. In some regions, it has been used to treat fever, headaches, and various types of pain. The plant's extracts have been studied for their antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties, which may contribute to its medicinal effects. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's potential benefits and risks. intro: Ipomoea corralinensis is a flowering plant species commonly known as "moonflower" or "calabash morning glory", native to tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its various health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of anxiety, insomnia, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Ipomoea corralinensis include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to promote relaxation, reduce stress, and improve sleep quality.
